text: In this educational video, we present the technical details of a rectovaginal fistula repair with episoprototomy and sphincteroplasty. A rectovaginal fistula developed after a vaginal delivery in a nine-year-old woman. Preoperative workup revealed an anteriorly located external anal sphincter defect. Procedure was performed in modified lithotomy positions under general anesthesia.

The surgeon removes the cetum after local marking with epinephrine injection. The skin is incised, and the sphincter complex is mobilized on both sides and divided across the fistula. Dissection proceeds along the perineal body, excising the fistula tract along the vagina until clean and healthy tissue is visible.

The surgeon performs a layered closure, first suturing the rectum, followed by the vagina, and placing intervening sutures between the levator muscles. The mobilized C-factor is overlapped and secured with two O-PDA sutures, with a total of four sutures placed.

The surgeon closes the rectal and vaginal mucosa with two O-vital sutures. A JP drain is positioned in the center of the wound and exits through a small stab incision in the left groin area. The skin along the perineal body is closed with three polysorb sutures. A vaginal tampon is placed into the vagina.
Functional and quality of life scores were good and there were no urination and defecation complaint at eight-week office visit.
